title: Per-cell vs centroid agree under same protocol; Entry 042 d=7.583 is PROTOCOL-DEPENDENT, not measurement-dependent

content.hypothesis: Entry 045 hypothesized that the VERY_NARROW d=7.583 reversal was due to per-cell vs centroid measurement methods. Entry 047 accepted this hypothesis and declared per-cell canonical. But neither controlled for PROTOCOL differences (Entry 042 used 50-step short protocol; validation used 300+60+30+60 long protocol). If per-cell and centroid are measured on the same organisms using the same protocol, do they agree?
content.method: Computed BOTH per-cell and centroid MI gap on every organism in a single run. Same long protocol as harness.py (300 warmup + 60/sig + 30 settle + 60 final). Multi-K [4,6,8,10], n_perm=8, n_trials=6. 7 clip conditions. TWO seed sets: SetA [10-19] and SetB [42,137,2024,999,7]. Cumulative training (shared organism). Script: src/stage4_percell_vs_centroid.py
content.result:
Per-cell and centroid give IDENTICAL rankings under the same protocol. VERY_NARROW ranks LAST (7/7) on BOTH measurements, BOTH seed sets.

SetA [10-19]:
  VERY_NARROW centroid rank: 7/7, per-cell rank: 7/7
  Per-cell/centroid ratio: 0.93-1.01x (essentially identical)
  VERY_NARROW vs CANONICAL: centroid d=-1.654, per-cell d=-1.965 (both strongly negative)

SetB [42,137,2024,999,7]:
  VERY_NARROW centroid rank: 7/7, per-cell rank: 7/7
  Per-cell/centroid ratio: 0.93-1.00x
  VERY_NARROW vs CANONICAL: centroid d=-1.733, per-cell d=-2.912 (both strongly negative)

Cross-seed-set comparison (long protocol):
  CANONICAL:    SetA cent=+0.1745, pc=+0.1709 | SetB cent=+0.1865, pc=+0.1738
  VERY_NARROW:  SetA cent=+0.0818, pc=+0.0814 | SetB cent=+0.0985, pc=+0.0947

Contrast with Entry 042/047 (SHORT protocol, per-cell only):
  CANONICAL short-protocol per-cell: +0.6274 (3.6x higher than long-protocol)
  VERY_NARROW short-protocol per-cell: +0.7657 (9.4x higher than long-protocol)

CONCLUSION: The d=7.583 result is entirely PROTOCOL-DEPENDENT. The short protocol (50 steps, no warmup/settle/final) produces both higher absolute MI values and reversed condition rankings compared to the long protocol (harness.py canonical). The per-cell vs centroid distinction is a red herring — they agree when protocol is held constant.

metrics.ranking_agreement: VERY_NARROW last (7/7) on ALL 4 combinations (2 methods × 2 seed sets)
metrics.protocol_confound: short protocol MI gap ~0.63-0.77; long protocol MI gap ~0.08-0.19 (4-9x difference)

content.methodology_lesson:
CRITICAL: When comparing measurement methods, PROTOCOL must be held constant. Entry 047 compared per-cell (short protocol) vs centroid (long protocol) and attributed the reversal to measurement method. The real confound was protocol length. This is a classic failure to control for confounds in a 2×2 design (method × protocol) — Entry 047 varied both simultaneously and attributed the effect to the wrong factor.

IMPLICATION FOR c028: Constraint c028 ('Always use per-cell cosine') is INVALID — it was derived from a confounded comparison. Both measurement methods give the same answer when protocol is controlled. The canonical protocol is harness.py (long protocol), and under it, both methods agree: VERY_NARROW is worst.
content.implication:
1. Entry 042's d=7.583 is real but PROTOCOL-SPECIFIC to the short (50-step) protocol.
2. Under the canonical harness.py protocol, clip bounds are NON-BINDING — c025 was correct.
3. Constraint c028 (per-cell canonical) is invalid and should be retracted.
4. The clip bound effect is TRANSIENT: it matters in early computation (short protocol) when alpha hasn't self-organized, but washes out with more training time (long protocol).
5. For Stage 4 purposes (harness.py protocol), clip bounds remain non-binding structural parameters.

constraints_implied: c029: Entry 042 d=7.583 for VERY_NARROW clip bounds is protocol-specific to the short (50-step) protocol and does not generalize to the canonical harness.py protocol
RETRACT c028: per-cell and centroid cosine give identical rankings under the same protocol; neither is inherently superior
